parsed.summary: Cumulative v0.4.0→v0.4.6+ diff removes a shared library module (process.mjs, 67 lines deleted), adds 203 lines of new retry logic (retry.mjs), introduces BigModel error dispatch (bigmodel-errors.mjs), and modifies state management and job tracking—all without visible proof that every runtime import path was updated or that the retry logic is bounded against storms. The deletion of a shared module alongside a companion import-check script modification strongly suggests the authors know this is a risk area but the check itself is not verified as exhaustive. This should not ship until the dead-import risk is confirmed eliminated and retry bounds are validated.

findings.title: process.mjs fully deleted — latent import paths will crash at runtime
findings.body: The diff stat shows `scripts/lib/process.mjs` losing 67 lines with zero additions, indicating complete file removal. The commit message 'Bundle F — ReferenceError + full thread/resume scaffolding purge' confirms this was a purge. However, `scripts/lib/job-control.mjs` (20 lines changed), `scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs` (78 lines changed), and `scripts/glm-companion.mjs` (139 lines changed) all overlap with the threading/resume domain. If any of these (or transitive consumers) still import from `process.mjs`, the runtime throws a module-not-found error that only surfaces in the affected code path—not on startup. The modification to `scripts/check-imports.mjs` (5 lines changed) is evidence the authors tried to validate this, but an import-check script cannot catch dynamic `import()` or re-eval at runtime, and the check script's own correctness is not verified.
findings.recommendation: Run `grep -r "process\.mjs" scripts/` and `grep -r "from.*process" scripts/` against the full tree including dynamic imports. Add a CI gate that fails if any reference to the deleted module path exists. Consider leaving a process.mjs that re-exports from the replacement module with a deprecation warning instead of a hard delete.

findings.title: 203-line retry.mjs added with unknown bounds — risk of retry storms and error masking
findings.body: `scripts/lib/retry.mjs` adds 203 lines of retry/backoff logic (the commit message says 'auto retry/backoff'). Without seeing the implementation, the default adversarial assumption is that (a) retry counts may be unbounded or too high for interactive use, (b) jitter may be absent causing thundering-herd on shared BigModel API quotas, (c) non-retryable error codes may be incorrectly classified as retryable, and (d) the retry wrapper may swallow the original error stack trace, making debugging impossible. The companion `bigmodel-errors.mjs` (168 lines) is the error classifier—its mapping of error codes to retryable/non-retryable determines whether the retry module does the right thing. A misclassification here silently wastes quota or hides permanent failures.
findings.recommendation: Verify retry.mjs has: (1) a hard maximum retry count (≤3 for user-facing, ≤5 for background), (2) exponential backoff with jitter, (3) a whitelist of retryable error codes (not a blacklist of non-retryable ones), (4) preservation of the original error cause chain, and (5) a circuit breaker or cooldown when consecutive failures exceed a threshold. Add integration tests that simulate persistent failures and assert the retry loop terminates.

findings.title: state.mjs and tracked-jobs.mjs file writes likely non-atomic — corruption on crash or concurrent access
findings.body: `scripts/lib/state.mjs` (32 lines changed) and `scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs` (78 lines changed) manage persistent state. File-based state without atomic writes (write-to-temp-then-rename) is vulnerable to corruption if the process is killed mid-write or if two invocations write concurrently. The job tracking domain is especially sensitive because a corrupted jobs file could cause orphaned background jobs, double-execution, or a stuck state that prevents future job submissions. The absence of any file-locking library in the diff supports this concern.
findings.recommendation: Audit state.mjs and tracked-jobs.mjs for write patterns. Every state file write should use the atomic write-to-temp-then-rename pattern: `fs.writeFile(tmpPath, data) → fs.rename(tmpPath, finalPath)`. If concurrent access is possible (multiple terminal sessions), add advisory file locking (e.g., proper-lockfile) or a PID-based mutex.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs error propagation gaps after retry wrapper integration
findings.body: `scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs` has 155 lines changed, likely integrating the new retry.mjs and bigmodel-errors.mjs modules. The risk is that the retry wrapper changes the error shape that callers expect—e.g., wrapping a network error in a RetryExhausted error that doesn't carry the same properties as the original. Callers in render.mjs (79 lines changed) and glm-companion.mjs may check error codes or messages that no longer match after wrapping. This is a classic silent-failure pattern where the error is caught, retried, and then re-thrown in a form the caller doesn't handle, leading to unhelpful 'unknown error' messages to the user.
findings.recommendation: Verify that retry.mjs preserves the original error's name, message, code, and stack when re-throwing after exhaustion. Add property-forwarding or use `error.cause` chaining. Write a test that asserts a retried-and-exhausted error is still instanceof the original error class and carries the original error code.

next_steps: Run `grep -rn 'process\.mjs\|from.*process' scripts/` to confirm zero dangling imports after the process.mjs deletion.
Audit retry.mjs for bounded retry count, jitter, retryable-error whitelist, and error cause preservation.
Verify state.mjs and tracked-jobs.mjs use atomic file writes (write-to-temp + rename).
Check that glm-client.mjs error objects survive the retry wrapper with correct type and properties.
Add an integration test that simulates persistent API failure through the full stack (client → retry → error dispatch → render) and asserts clean termination with actionable error output.
